questions.question: Was the 64-session quota directly observed rather than inferred from a control-plane value?
questions.evidence: The balanced 32 EN + 32 ZH probe observed 64 simultaneously active sessions for 35 samples, from 21.383 to 55.396 seconds after launch.

questions.question: Did the task-shaped 28 EN + 36 ZH allocation reliably reach 64 active sessions?
questions.evidence: Both attempts failed to reach 64; their observed active-session peaks were 49 and 46.

questions.question: Do those failures prove a fixed total limit of 49 or a ZH limit of 25?
questions.evidence: 25 EN + 25 ZH, 32 EN + 32 ZH, and ZH-only 36 all succeeded, so the evidence points to high-water allocation or timing instability rather than those fixed caps.

questions.question: Did the formal Lexmount c32 arm actually observe 32 concurrent sessions?
questions.evidence: The c32 session monitor recorded active-session p95 = 32 and max = 32. The analyzer now requires the observed maximum to reach the named concurrency before marking a Lexmount arm sustainable.

questions.question: Did every formal c16 and c32 arm produce a complete evaluation?
questions.evidence: All four arms recorded 64 planned, 64 trajectories, and 64 valid Judge records; synthetic Judge failures remaining were 0.

questions.question: Did either formal backend fail session creation or trigger a memory guard?
questions.evidence: All four arms had session_create = 0 and guard_triggered = null. The lowest host MemAvailable observation was 48.3038 GiB, above the 32 GiB guard.

questions.question: Did Lexmount leak browser sessions at c16 or c32?
questions.evidence: Both monitors started at 0 and ended at 0 active sessions, with residual 0 and monitor error count 0.

questions.question: Did the first failed detached c16 launch contaminate a formal arm?
questions.evidence: The launch failed before the user cgroup scope and before any task or browser session; it produced 0 trajectories and 0 residual sessions and is disclosed separately in launch-recovery.json.

questions.question: Does c32 prove Lexmount and Local have equal quality?
questions.evidence: The point estimate was 40/64 versus 41/64, but the paired 95% bootstrap interval for Lexmount minus Local was [-14.0625, +10.9375] percentage points and did not pass the 5-point noninferiority rule.

questions.question: Can the c16-to-c32 quality changes be attributed to concurrency?
questions.evidence: Each concurrency has one run per backend. The observed Lexmount change was -5/64 and Local was +3/64, but there are no within-concurrency replicates.

questions.question: Are the resource values end-to-end browser-system costs?
questions.evidence: Local includes runner, Agent, and local Chrome. Lexmount includes runner and Agent controller only and excludes remote browser hosts.

questions.question: Can the measured GPU variation be attributed to either browser backend?
questions.evidence: GPT-5.5 was served through a remote API and unrelated GPU workloads existed on the shared host, so GPU measurements are context only.

questions.question: Why was a formal c64 Agent arm not launched?
questions.answer: the admission gate did not pass
questions.evidence: The task-shaped 28 EN + 36 ZH raw admission test failed twice. Launching a named c64 arm would risk reporting configured rather than observed concurrency.

questions.question: What is the strongest next falsification test?
questions.evidence: Require at least two consecutive successful 28 EN + 36 ZH preflights, or provider-side reservation/allocation evidence, before running repeated formal c64 arms.
